transcript:
[00:00:01] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Hey everyone, thanks for jumping on. I know we've got a full group today so I want to be respectful of time. Let me just confirm — Marcus, Linda, Priya, you're all on?

[00:00:09] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): Yep, I'm here. Good to see everyone.

[00:00:11] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): I'm here, hi Rachel.

[00:00:13] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Morning. I'm on too.

[00:00:15] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Great. And I've got Jake Torres on from our side — he's our Head of Delivery, so he can speak to timeline, process, team composition, all of that.

[00:00:22] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Hey everyone. Good to finally put voices to names.

[00:00:25] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): Jake, good to have you. I think you're the person who's going to tell us whether we can actually pull this off before BuildCon.

[00:00:31] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): That's the plan. I've been reviewing the scope doc Rachel put together and I've got some thoughts on that.

[00:00:36] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Okay so just to set context — I know Tom was on the demo last week and he seemed, you know, very positive about the direction. He mentioned that Linda would be the one to work through the numbers with us. So I thought today would be a good opportunity to, um, get into the details — pricing, scope, timeline, contract terms — and see if we can find a structure that works for everyone. Does that feel right?

[00:00:58] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): That sounds right. I'll be honest, Rachel — I did review your proposal over the weekend. The work looks impressive, the demo was strong, but the number is... it's a stretch for us. I want to find a path forward, but I want to be upfront about that.

[00:01:13] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): I appreciate that, Linda. Honestly, that's exactly why I wanted to have this conversation. The proposal at eighty-five is our, you know, full-scope recommendation, but I know there are levers we can pull. So let's dig in.

[00:01:25] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): And just — before we get too deep into the numbers — I want to make sure everyone remembers the BuildCon piece. We've got, what, about three and a half months? Our current site is... I mean, Priya, you've seen the analytics. It's embarrassing.

[00:01:39] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): It's not great. We're running on a WordPress theme from 2019 that nobody on my team wants to touch. The page load times are over six seconds on mobile. I actually ran a Lighthouse audit last week and we scored a twenty-two on performance.

[00:01:53] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Right, and I understand the urgency. I'm not questioning whether we need a new site. I'm questioning whether we need an eighty-five thousand dollar site.

[00:02:02] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Fair. So let me ask — Linda, what's the budget range you're working with? I'd rather know where we are than dance around it.

[00:02:09] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): We budgeted forty to fifty for the website project this fiscal year. Tom and I talked after the demo and there's some flexibility, but... fifty was the number we planned around.

[00:02:21] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Okay. Yeah, so there's a gap there, I won't pretend there isn't. But I think — Jake, maybe you can help here — I think if we look at what absolutely needs to go live before BuildCon versus what could come in a second phase, we can probably find something that works.

[00:02:37] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Yeah, totally. So I've been looking at the scope doc and here's how I'd break it down. The core website — the homepage, about page, services pages, project portfolio, contact — that's your bread and butter. That's what you need live for BuildCon. Then there's the more complex stuff: the careers page with the Greenhouse ATS integration, the client portal concept, the interactive project estimator tool that was in the proposal. Those are all great features but they're also where a lot of the engineering hours live.

[00:03:08] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Can you break down the hours on the ATS integration specifically? Because I've worked with Greenhouse's API before and it can be... finicky.

[00:03:16] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): You're not wrong. We scoped that at about eighty hours, which includes the API integration, the custom application flow, the admin filtering dashboard on your side, and testing. Greenhouse's API is fine for pulling job listings, but the application submission flow has some quirks, especially around resume parsing and custom fields.

[00:03:35] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Yeah, we have a bunch of custom fields for certifications and trade specialties. That's going to be a whole thing.

[00:03:41] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Exactly. And honestly? That's the kind of integration that benefits from not being rushed. If we carve that out as phase two, we can do it right instead of cramming it into an already tight timeline.

[00:03:52] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Okay, so if we pull the careers integration and the client portal out, what does that do to the number?

[00:03:59] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): So the careers slash ATS integration is about fifteen K of the total. The client portal concept was another eight. If we move both to phase two, we're looking at... roughly sixty-two for the core site.

[00:04:14] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Sixty-two. That's closer. But I'm going to be honest, I was hoping to land somewhere around sixty.

[00:04:21] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Mm. Yeah. Um. So sixty is... let me think about what that looks like. Because there are some things in the core scope I don't want to cut because they'll directly impact how the site performs for you.

[00:04:34] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): And I want to be careful here too, Linda. If we cut too deep on the core site, we're going to launch something at BuildCon that doesn't actually move the needle. We're spending twelve thousand dollars on the booth and sponsorship. If someone visits our site after meeting us there and it looks like a template...

[00:04:50] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): I hear you, Marcus. I'm not trying to build a cheap site. I'm trying to make sure we're being responsible with the spend. We're also looking at new equipment purchases this quarter, so every dollar matters.

[00:05:01] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Linda, what if we did this. What if we keep the full core scope at sixty-two but we tie it to a retainer commitment? If you commit to a twelve-month retainer, I can bring the project fee down to... let me see... seventy-two for the full eighty-five K scope, or if we're doing the phased approach, I could come down to fifty-eight on phase one.

[00:05:24] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Wait, walk me through that again. Fifty-eight for the core site if we commit to the retainer for twelve months?

[00:05:31] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Right. Well — okay, let me back up. My thinking is this. The retainer is two thousand a month in the proposal. If you commit to twelve months, I can bring it down to fifteen hundred a month, and I can take a few K off the project fee because we have the guaranteed recurring revenue. So it'd be... the core site for BuildCon at, let's call it sixty, with the retainer at fifteen hundred a month for twelve months.

[00:05:56] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): So sixty for the project, plus eighteen thousand for the annual retainer. Seventy-eight total first-year cost.

[00:06:04] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): For, yeah, for the first year all-in. And then the retainer continues at fifteen hundred after that, month to month, cancel with thirty days notice after the initial commitment.

[00:06:13] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): And what's in the retainer? Because two thousand a month felt high to me even before the discount.

[00:06:19] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Sure. So the retainer covers hosting on our managed infrastructure, which is Vercel for the frontend, your CMS hosting — we're recommending Sanity — security updates, SSL management, uptime monitoring, performance monitoring, and then ten hours of content updates per month. So if you need to swap out project photos, update a services page, add a blog post, change team bios — that's all covered.

[00:06:44] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Can we do the content updates in-house? If we skip that piece, what does the retainer look like?

[00:06:50] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): I'd push back on that actually, Linda. My team is maxed out on the ERP migration and the field reporting app. I don't want to commit dev time to website content changes. That's exactly the kind of stuff we should be paying someone else to handle.

[00:07:04] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Can't the marketing team handle content updates? Marcus?

[00:07:08] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): If it's a CMS with a good editing experience, some of it, yeah. But we don't have anyone who can touch code. And there's always stuff that goes beyond just typing in a text box — layout changes, adding new sections, that kind of thing.

[00:07:22] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): I'll add — with Sanity as the CMS, your marketing team can absolutely handle basic content updates. Blog posts, swapping images, editing text. The structured content model makes that pretty intuitive. Where the retainer hours really come in is things like adding a new page template, updating the site's schema, performance optimization as your traffic grows, and honestly just having someone who knows the codebase available when you need them.

[00:07:47] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Jake, can I ask — why Sanity over something like Contentful or Strapi?

[00:07:52] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Yeah, good question. We've used all three extensively. Sanity wins for us on a few fronts. The real-time collaboration is great if you have multiple content editors. The GROQ query language is more intuitive than GraphQL for content operations, in my opinion. The pricing model is better for mid-size companies — Contentful gets expensive fast once you go beyond their community tier. And the customizable studio means we can build an editing experience that actually matches how your team thinks about content, not force you into a generic interface.

[00:08:21] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): And you're using Next.js for the frontend? App Router?

[00:08:24] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Yes, Next.js 14 with the App Router. Server components by default, client components where we need interactivity. We deploy on Vercel, which gives us automatic preview deployments for content changes, edge caching, image optimization — the whole stack is optimized to work together.

[00:08:40] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): What about if we ever want to move off Vercel? I don't love being locked into a hosting platform.

[00:08:46] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Totally valid concern. Next.js can be self-hosted — you can run it on a Node server, Docker container, wherever. We've done migrations off Vercel for other clients. It's not zero effort, but it's not a rewrite either. The code is standard Next.js, no proprietary Vercel APIs or anything. You'd lose some of the deployment convenience, but the app itself is portable.

[00:09:05] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Okay, good. That matters to me. We got burned by a vendor lock-in situation with our old CRM and I don't want to repeat that.

[00:09:12] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): I want to come back to the numbers, if that's okay. So we're at sixty for the project, fifteen hundred a month retainer. Rachel, I'm going to push you a little here. Is sixty your floor on the project?

[00:09:24] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Um. Yeah, effectively. I mean, at sixty we're already making some concessions on our margins. We could go lower, but then I'd need to start trimming the core scope — and I don't think that serves you well. Like, we could drop the number of unique page designs from eight to five, but then your services pages are going to look generic. We could skip the custom animation work, but that's a lot of what made the demo feel premium.

[00:09:49] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): What if we met at... fifty-eight for the project and kept the retainer at fifteen hundred?

[00:09:55] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Fifty-eight is... that's tough, Linda. Let me ask this — is there flexibility on the retainer term? What if instead of twelve months, you committed to eighteen? That gives us more revenue certainty and I might be able to make fifty-eight work on the project side.

[00:10:11] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Eighteen months is a long commitment. We don't even know if we'll be happy with the work yet.

[00:10:16] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Right, and I understand that. We could include an out clause — if you're not satisfied after six months, you can terminate the retainer with sixty days notice. But the commitment helps us plan resources and frankly, it's what lets me discount the project fee.

[00:10:30] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Priya, Marcus — thoughts on eighteen months?

[00:10:34] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): I'm fine with it. If the site is good, we're going to need ongoing support anyway. And honestly, eighteen months takes us past BuildCon, past our next fiscal year planning — it just makes sense from a continuity standpoint.

[00:10:46] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): As long as there's that out clause Rachel mentioned, I don't see the risk. If they do a bad job, we leave. If they do a good job, we'd stay anyway.

[00:10:54] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Alright. Fifty-eight for the project, fifteen hundred a month for eighteen months, with a termination option after six. Let me do the math... that's fifty-eight plus twenty-seven thousand for the retainer. Eighty-five total over eighteen months. Hmm. That's actually... that tracks close to your original proposal but spread out differently.

[00:11:14] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): It's structured in a way that's easier to budget for, though, right? The big spend is fifty-eight upfront for the project, and then the retainer is a predictable monthly line item.

[00:11:23] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): It is. I can work with predictable monthly costs. Okay — I'm not saying yes yet, but I'm not saying no. Let's keep going. Jake, talk to me about the timeline. Three and a half months. Is this realistic?

[00:11:36] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): For the phased scope — the core site without the ATS integration and client portal — yes, it's realistic. Here's how I'd break it down. Weeks one through two: discovery and content strategy. We do stakeholder interviews, audit your existing content, define the information architecture, establish brand guidelines for the digital experience. We'd need access to your brand assets, any photography you have, that kind of thing.

[00:12:01] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): We just did a rebrand six months ago, so we've got a full brand guide, new photography, the works. That should speed things up.

[00:12:08] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): That helps a lot, actually. Most of the discovery delays we see are when companies don't have their brand assets together. Okay, so weeks three and four: design phase. We'll do homepage and two key interior pages as high-fidelity mockups in Figma. Full review cycle with your team. We typically do two rounds of revisions. By the end of week four, we need design approval to stay on track.

[00:12:30] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): What happens if we're slow on approvals? I know how our team works — getting five people to agree on a color takes a week.

[00:12:38] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Ha, yeah. So we build in three business days for each review round. If it takes longer, the timeline shifts. That's probably the biggest risk to hitting BuildCon, honestly — not the build, but the approval cycles. So I'd recommend identifying one or two decision-makers for design approvals. Doesn't have to be the whole leadership team on every pixel.

[00:12:57] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): That can be me. I'll own design approvals. Tom already said he trusts my judgment on this as long as it looks modern and professional.

[00:13:05] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Perfect. Okay, weeks five through nine: development. We build in two-week sprints. First sprint is the component library and homepage. Second sprint is interior pages and the CMS integration. Third sprint... well, it's a partial sprint — final pages, polish, animation work. We do weekly demos so you can see progress in real time. Nothing's a surprise at the end.

[00:13:28] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): What's your team composition look like for this? Who's actually building it?

[00:13:32] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Good question. For a project this size, we'd have a lead developer — full-stack, very strong with Next.js and React — plus a junior developer for component work and content integration. A dedicated designer for the first four weeks who stays available for UI tweaks during development. A project manager who runs the weekly syncs and is your day-to-day point of contact. And I'm overseeing the technical architecture and doing code reviews.

[00:13:57] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Who's the lead dev? Can you share anything about their background?

[00:14:01] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Yeah — her name's Amara. She's been with us for three years, came from a fintech background. She's built about a dozen Next.js sites for us, including two in the construction and real estate space. Very sharp, very detail-oriented. She'd be on this full time.

[00:14:16] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Can I see any of those construction sites? Just to get a feel for the quality.

[00:14:20] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Absolutely. I'll send you links after this call. One of them is actually a pretty similar scope — multi-page corporate site with a project portfolio and Sanity CMS.

[00:14:29] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Great. And — this might be premature — but I want to talk about code access. Who owns the code at the end of the project?

[00:14:37] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Full IP transfer to NovaBuild on final payment. The codebase, the design files, all custom components, the CMS schema — everything we build is yours.

[00:14:46] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Good. But I don't want to wait until the end. Can we have access to the Git repo during development? I want my team to be able to look at the code as it's being written. Not to interfere — just to understand what's being built and how.

[00:14:59] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Yeah, we can do that. We'll set you up on a shared GitHub repo. You'd have read access from day one of development, and we can bump that to write access at handoff. Does that work?

[00:15:09] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Perfect. Read access during dev is fine. I just don't want a black box where we get a zip file at the end and hope for the best.

[00:15:16] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Completely understand. We're big on transparency. You'll be able to see every commit, every PR, the whole history.

[00:15:23] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Alright, I want to talk about payment terms. Your proposal says forty percent upfront, which on fifty-eight K is... twenty-three thousand two hundred. That's a big check to write before we've seen any work.

[00:15:35] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Yeah, so our standard terms are forty percent upfront, thirty at midpoint, thirty at launch, Net 30 on each invoice. The upfront covers resource allocation — we're pulling a full-time developer off other projects, the designer, the PM. It's about guaranteeing capacity.

[00:15:51] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): I'd like to restructure that. Forty percent upfront is too heavy for us. What about twenty-five percent to start?

[00:15:58] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Twenty-five is a little low for us to comfortably commit the team. What about thirty? That's... seventeen thousand four hundred. And then we do forty percent at design approval — because that's a real milestone, you've signed off on the direction — and then the remaining thirty at launch.

[00:16:15] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): So thirty, forty, thirty. And what are the payment terms? We typically work on Net 60 with our vendors.

[00:16:22] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Net 60 is tough for us, Linda. We're a twenty-person shop, not a Fortune 500. Cash flow matters. Net 30 is our standard. Could we meet in the middle at Net 45?

[00:16:33] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Net 45... yeah, I can make Net 45 work. Our AP team won't love it, but I'll push it through.

[00:16:39] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Great. So to recap the payment structure: thirty percent on contract signing, that's seventeen four. Forty percent on design approval, that's twenty-three two. Thirty percent at launch, seventeen four. All Net 45.

[00:16:54] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): And the retainer? When does that start?

[00:16:57] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): The retainer kicks in at launch. So you're not paying that until the site is live.

[00:17:02] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Good. That's fair.

[00:17:05] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): Can I jump in on the timeline piece again? Jake, you mentioned weeks five through nine for development. Then what?

[00:17:12] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Good catch, I skipped ahead. Weeks ten through eleven: QA and content loading. We do cross-browser testing, mobile testing on actual devices — not just responsive views in Chrome — accessibility testing to WCAG 2.1 AA, performance optimization. We're targeting a Lighthouse score north of ninety on both mobile and desktop. And your team would be loading final content into the CMS during this period, with our support.

[00:17:37] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): WCAG 2.1 AA — is that included or is that an add-on?

[00:17:41] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Included. We build with accessibility in mind from the start — semantic HTML, proper ARIA labels, keyboard navigation, color contrast ratios. It's not a bolt-on at the end.

[00:17:51] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Good. We've had some legal inquiries about ADA compliance on our current site, so that's important.

[00:17:57] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Yeah, a lot of construction companies are getting hit with that. Smart to get ahead of it. Okay, and then week twelve through about week thirteen: staging review, final revisions, and launch. We do a soft launch first — site goes live but we watch performance, check for issues. Then a hard cutover where we update DNS and retire the old site. We'd aim to have you live at least two weeks before BuildCon so there's a buffer.

[00:18:20] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): Two weeks of buffer. I like that. That also gives us time to update all our collateral — business cards, email signatures, everything that points to the website.

[00:18:29] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): What if the timeline slips? What's the contingency?

[00:18:33] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): So, a couple of things. First, we build the schedule with some padding already. The development phase has buffer built in. Second, if things slip due to our team, we absorb it — we'll add resources if needed. If it slips because of delayed approvals or content on your side, we'll flag it early and work with you to get back on track, but we can't own that risk entirely.

[00:18:54] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Is there any kind of penalty clause if you miss the launch date?

[00:18:58] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): We don't typically do penalty clauses, Linda. What I can tell you is that in the last two years, we've hit our launch date on ninety percent of projects. The ten percent that slipped were all due to client-side delays. What we can put in the contract is a commitment to the launch date and a clause that says if we miss it due to our own delays, we'll provide the first two months of the retainer at no cost. That's three thousand dollars in value.

[00:19:23] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): That's something. I'd want that in writing.

[00:19:26] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Absolutely, we'll include it in the contract.

[00:19:29] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): I think that's fair. And honestly, the biggest risk on timing is going to be us, not them. I've been on the other side of this — the client is almost always the bottleneck on approvals.

[00:19:39] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Which is why I'm glad Marcus is owning design approvals. Okay. Let me ask about something else. The phase two work — the careers page with Greenhouse integration and the client portal. If we do that, what's the timeline and cost?

[00:19:53] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): The ATS integration we scoped at fifteen thousand. The client portal was another eight. If you did both, we'd do twenty-one for the bundle — so a small discount for packaging them together. Timeline would be about six to eight weeks after phase one, and we could start that whenever you're ready — doesn't have to be immediately.

[00:20:12] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): So if I'm looking at total cost to get everything in the original proposal, it's fifty-eight plus twenty-one... seventy-nine. Plus the retainer. Actually that's better than the original eighty-five.

[00:20:23] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Right, and you're spreading it out. The fifty-eight hits this quarter, the twenty-one hits next quarter or whenever you're ready. It's easier to budget.

[00:20:31] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Hmm. Yeah. Okay, I actually — from a fiscal planning standpoint, I like that better. Tom always wants to spread capex when we can.

[00:20:40] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Rachel, Jake — one more technical question. How do you handle environments? Like, do we get a staging environment for testing before things go to production?

[00:20:49] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Yes. So with Vercel, every pull request automatically generates a preview deployment with a unique URL. So you'll be able to see exactly what a change looks like in a production-equivalent environment before it merges. On top of that, we maintain a dedicated staging environment that mirrors production. Any content changes you make in the CMS can be previewed before publishing too — Sanity has a built-in preview mode that we hook into Next.js.

[00:21:14] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): And you said you're using the App Router. Are you doing ISR or full SSR or...?

[00:21:19] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Mix, depending on the page type. For content pages — your services, about, projects — we use ISR with on-demand revalidation. So when someone updates content in Sanity, it triggers a webhook that revalidates just that page. The user gets a static page from the cache, so it's fast, but the content is always current. For anything dynamic — like, if phase two includes the client portal — that would be server-rendered per request with proper auth.

[00:21:45] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Okay, yeah. That's how I'd do it too. What about — sorry, one more — what about the headless WordPress question? I know some agencies push headless WordPress as a CMS because clients are familiar with it. Why not that route?

[00:21:58] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): Honestly? We've done headless WordPress and it works, but it's... a lot of overhead for what you get. You're still running a full WordPress installation somewhere — PHP, MySQL, plugins, updates, security patches. All that backend complexity for what is essentially a content API. Sanity gives you the content management without all that baggage. It's purpose-built to be headless. The developer experience is better, the content modeling is more flexible, and you're not inheriting the security surface area of WordPress.

[00:22:27] Priya Sharma (CTO, NovaBuild): Yeah, that's my read too. I've been pushing to get off WordPress for two years. I don't want to end up back on it even in a headless configuration. I just wanted to make sure you weren't going to try to sell me on it.

[00:22:37] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): No, definitely not. We've been moving away from WordPress for anything beyond basic brochure sites. The modern stack — Next.js, Sanity, Vercel — it's just a better foundation for what you're trying to build.

[00:22:48] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): Can I ask a marketing question while you two are nerding out? What about SEO? Our current site barely ranks for anything. We're on page three for "commercial construction company" in our metro area.

[00:23:00] Jake Torres (Head of Delivery, Velocity Digital): So, a few things. The performance improvements alone will help — Google's Core Web Vitals are a ranking factor, and going from a six-second load time to under two seconds is significant. We build with SEO fundamentals: proper heading hierarchy, meta tags, structured data markup, XML sitemaps, clean URLs. We'll also set up proper redirects from your old URL structure so you don't lose whatever authority you've built.

[00:23:24] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): We don't include ongoing SEO strategy — like keyword research, content planning for search, link building — that's a different discipline. But the technical foundation will be rock solid.

[00:23:34] Marcus Webb (VP Marketing, NovaBuild): Yeah, we have an SEO consultant we work with. She's been complaining about the current site for months. She'll be thrilled to have a technically sound platform to work with.

[00:23:43] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Okay. I want to take stock of where we are. Rachel, can you summarize the deal as it stands right now?

[00:23:50] Rachel Chen (AE, Velocity Digital): Sure. Phase one: core website redesign and build, everything except the ATS integration and client portal, delivered before BuildCon with a two-week buffer. Price: fifty-eight thousand. Payment structure: thirty percent on signing — seventeen four — forty percent on design approval — twenty-three two — thirty percent on launch — seventeen four — all Net 45. Retainer: fifteen hundred a month, eighteen-month commitment starting at launch, with an out clause after six months on sixty days notice. Git repo access for your team during development. Full IP transfer on final payment. Launch date guarantee with two months free retainer if we miss it. Phase two: careers page with Greenhouse integration and client portal, twenty-one thousand, timeline TBD.

[00:24:38] Linda Zhao (CFO, NovaBuild): Priya, are you comfortable with the scope for phase one? I want to make sure we're not cutting something critical.
